theorem_statement:
Let \(p\) and \(q\) be monic real-rooted polynomials of degree \(n\), and let \(p\boxplus_n q\) denote their finite free additive convolution. For a monic real-rooted polynomial \(p\) with simple roots \(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n\), define its score vector by
\[
s(p)_i=\sum_{j\ne i}\frac{1}{\lambda_i-\lambda_j},
\]
and define its finite free Fisher information by
\[
\Phi(p)=\sum_{i=1}^n s(p)_i^2.
\]
If \(p\) has multiple roots, set \(\Phi(p)=+\infty\). Then
\[
\frac{1}{\Phi(p\boxplus_n q)}
\ge
\frac{1}{\Phi(p)}+\frac{1}{\Phi(q)},
\]
with the convention \(1/\infty=0\).
raw_proof_text:
It is enough first to prove the theorem when all relevant polynomials have simple roots. The general case follows by approximation. One perturbs a monic real-rooted polynomial by a small differential operator \(T_\varepsilon\) which preserves degree and real-rootedness and makes the roots simple for \(\varepsilon>0\). The perturbations converge back to the original polynomial as \(\varepsilon\to0\), and the perturbation is compatible with finite free convolution. Thus, after proving the inequality for the perturbed simple-rooted polynomials and letting \(\varepsilon\to0\), the inequality follows for the original polynomials by continuity of the roots away from collisions and lower semicontinuity of the Fisher information at collisions.

Assume now that \(p\), \(q\), and \(p\boxplus_n q\) have simple real roots. Let
\[
x=(x_1,
\dots,
x_n),\qquad y=(y_1,
\dots,
y_n),
\qquad z=(z_1,
\dots,
z_n)
\]
be the ordered root vectors of \(p\), \(q\), and \(p\boxplus_n q\). Near the simple-root locus, finite free convolution gives a smooth map
\[
F(x,y)=z.
\]
Let \(J_x\) and \(J_y\) be the Jacobian matrices of \(F\) with respect to the variables \(x\) and \(y\).

We next identify the score vector as an infinitesimal root motion. Let \(p_t\) be the reverse-heat deformation of \(p\), and let \(x_i(t)\) be the root of \(p_t\) starting at \(x_i\). Since
\[
p_t(x_i(t))=0,
\]
differentiating at \(t=0\) gives
\[
\partial_t p_t(x_i)+p'(x_i)x_i'(0)=0.
\]
The reverse-heat equation expresses \(\partial_t p_t\) as a constant multiple of \(p''\). At a simple root \(x_i\), the logarithmic derivative computation gives
\[
\frac{p''(x_i)}{p'(x_i)}=2\sum_{j\ne i}\frac{1}{x_i-x_j}.
\]
With the normalization of the reverse-heat flow used in the definition of finite free Fisher information, this gives
\[
x_i'(0)=s(p)_i.
\]
Thus the score vector \(s(p)\) is the velocity of the root vector of \(p\) under reverse heat flow. The same statement holds for \(q\) and for \(p\boxplus_n q\).

Finite free convolution commutes with the heat-flow deformation. Therefore, deforming \(p\) and then convolving with \(q\) gives the same infinitesimal root motion of \(p\boxplus_n q\) as applying the Jacobian \(J_x\) to the root velocity of \(p\). Similarly, deforming \(q\) gives the Jacobian \(J_y\). Consequently the score vector of the convolution is related to the score vectors of \(p\) and \(q\) by
\[
s(p\boxplus_n q)=J_xs(p)=J_ys(q)
\]
in the common heat-flow direction. Equivalently, for any \(\alpha\in[0,1]\), the same vector can be written as the convex combination
\[
s(p\boxplus_n q)=\alpha J_xs(p)+(1-\alpha)J_ys(q).
\]

The central analytic input is a Jacobian contraction inequality. It says that for all vectors \(u,v\) in the relevant mean-zero tangent space and all \(\alpha\in[0,1]\),
\[
\left|\alpha J_xu+(1-\alpha)J_yv\right|^2
\le
\alpha^2|u|^2+(1-\alpha)^2|v|^2.
\]
This inequality follows from the convexity theory of hyperbolic polynomials. In outline, one studies the coordinate functions of the convolution root map \(F\), computes their Hessians using variance additivity and the differential identities of finite free convolution, and obtains a positive-semidefinite matrix inequality for the Hessian form. This positive-semidefinite inequality is exactly the statement that the Jacobians contract quadratic forms in the displayed way.

Applying the Jacobian contraction inequality with \(u=s(p)\) and \(v=s(q)\) gives
\[
\Phi(p\boxplus_n q)
=|s(p\boxplus_n q)|^2
\le
\alpha^2|s(p)|^2+(1-\alpha)^2|s(q)|^2
=
\alpha^2\Phi(p)+(1-\alpha)^2\Phi(q).
\]
This holds for every \(\alpha\in[0,1]\).

It remains to minimize the right side. Let \(A=\Phi(p)\) and \(B=\Phi(q)\). The elementary minimization problem
\[
\min_{0\le\alpha\le1}\bigl(\alpha^2A+(1-\alpha)^2B\bigr)
\]
has minimizer \(\alpha=B/(A+B)\), provided \(A,B>0\), and the minimum value is
\[
\frac{AB}{A+B}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi(p\boxplus_n q)\le \frac{\Phi(p)\Phi(q)}{\Phi(p)+\Phi(q)}.
\]
Taking reciprocals gives
\[
\frac{1}{\Phi(p\boxplus_n q)}
\ge
\frac{1}{\Phi(p)}+\frac{1}{\Phi(q)}.
\]
The cases where one of the Fisher informations is infinite follow by the approximation argument at the beginning, with the convention \(1/\infty=0\). This proves the finite free Stam inequality.
